sections.title: Python Basics & Syntax
sections.content: Python is a high-level, interpreted, general-purpose language known for simple syntax and readability. It uses indentation to define blocks, so spaces and tabs matter. Unlike Java or C, Python does not use braces for blocks. Python is dynamically typed, meaning a variable can refer to different data types at different times. It is also strongly typed, so Python does not silently mix incompatible types in most cases. Everything in Python is an object, including numbers, functions, and classes. The interpreter executes code line by line, which is why Python is widely used for scripting, automation, web development, data science, and AI. Important syntax facts: comments use #, docstrings use triple quotes, and colons are used after if, for, while, def, class, and try blocks. Python is case-sensitive. Variable names can contain letters, digits, and underscores, but cannot start with a digit. Common keywords include if, elif, else, for, while, def, class, import, return, try, except, finally, lambda, break, continue, pass, and global. MCQ traps: indentation errors are syntax errors; Python has no semicolon requirement; and assignment uses =, while comparison uses ==.

sections.title: Data Types & Structures
sections.content: Python has several built-in data types. Numeric types include int, float, and complex. Boolean values are True and False. Text is stored as str, which is immutable. Core built-in containers are list, tuple, set, and dict. List is ordered, mutable, and allows duplicates. It is commonly used when the sequence may change. Tuple is ordered, immutable, and can also contain duplicates. It is often used for fixed data. Set is unordered, mutable, and stores only unique elements. Set operations like union, intersection, and difference are common. Dictionary stores key-value pairs and preserves insertion order in modern Python versions. Dictionary keys must be hashable, so mutable objects like lists cannot be keys. None represents the absence of a value and is used for empty returns or missing data. Useful conversions: int(), float(), str(), list(), tuple(), set(), dict(). Important concepts: mutability affects whether an object can be changed in place; hashability is required for set elements and dict keys. Some sequence types support slicing using [start:stop:step]. Negative indexing counts from the end. MCQ traps: {} is an empty dictionary, not a set; empty set is created using set(); strings and tuples are immutable; lists are mutable.

sections.title: Control Flow
sections.content: Python uses if, elif, and else for decision-making. Conditions must evaluate to True or False. Indentation controls the block under each condition. Nested if statements are also allowed. The for loop iterates over an iterable such as a list, string, tuple, dictionary, range object, or set. The while loop repeats as long as the condition remains true. break exits the nearest loop immediately. continue skips the rest of the current iteration and moves to the next one. pass is a no-operation statement used when syntax requires a block but no action is needed. range(stop), range(start, stop), and range(start, stop, step) are commonly used in for loops. Python also supports loop else. The else block runs only if the loop ends normally and no break statement was executed. Membership operators in and not in are often used in control-flow conditions. MCQ traps: for-loop else is not the same as if-else; break prevents loop else from running; and while loops may become infinite if the condition never changes.

sections.title: Functions & Scope
sections.content: Functions are defined using def and used to organize reusable logic. A function may take parameters and may return a value using return. If return is omitted, the function returns None by default. Python supports positional arguments, keyword arguments, default arguments, variable-length positional arguments (*args), and variable-length keyword arguments (**kwargs). A lambda is an anonymous one-line function, often used with map, filter, and sorted. Variable scope in Python is commonly explained using LEGB: Local, Enclosing, Global, Built-in. A variable defined inside a function is local to that function. The global keyword allows modification of a global variable inside a function. The nonlocal keyword is used in nested functions to refer to a variable in the nearest enclosing scope that is not global. Python uses pass-by-object-reference, which means the function receives a reference to the object, not a copy of the variable name itself. Default arguments are evaluated once at function definition time, which creates a common trap with mutable defaults such as lists. MCQ traps: using a mutable default like [] can preserve values across calls; lambda is limited to a single expression; and return ends function execution immediately.

sections.title: OOP in Python
sections.content: Python supports object-oriented programming through classes and objects. A class is a blueprint, and an object is an instance of that class. The __init__ method acts as a constructor and initializes object state. self refers to the current instance and is used to access instance variables and methods. Python supports encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, and abstraction, although its access control is by convention rather than strict enforcement. Single inheritance and multiple inheritance are both supported. Method overriding is possible when a subclass defines a method with the same name as its parent class. Method overloading is not supported in the same way as Java; Python typically uses default arguments or variable arguments to simulate it. Instance variables belong to objects, while class variables are shared by all instances of the class. Special methods like __str__, __repr__, __len__, __add__, and __eq__ customize object behavior and operator overloading. Public members have no leading underscore. A single leading underscore is a convention for protected-like members, while double leading underscores trigger name mangling. MCQ traps: Python does not have strict private access modifiers; class variables are shared; and __init__ is not a true constructor in the C++ sense, but an initializer.

sections.title: Modules & Packages
sections.content: A module is a Python file containing code such as functions, variables, or classes. A package is a collection of modules organized in folders. Modules are imported using import module_name or from module_name import something. Aliasing with as is also common. The __name__ variable helps identify whether a file is being run directly or imported. If __name__ == '__main__', the code is executed as the main program. This is a very common MCQ topic because it controls whether test code or demo code runs on import. Python has a large standard library, including math, random, os, sys, datetime, collections, statistics, json, and re. The import system searches through paths listed in sys.path. Packages can contain __init__.py, though modern Python packaging may not always require it in every context. MCQ traps: importing a module executes its top-level code once; from module import * is legal but discouraged because it pollutes the namespace; and aliasing helps avoid long names or name conflicts.

sections.title: File Handling & I/O
sections.content: File handling in Python uses the open() function, which returns a file object. The most common modes are r for reading, w for writing, a for appending, and x for exclusive creation. Binary modes include rb, wb, and ab. Text mode is the default. Reading methods include read(), readline(), and readlines(). Writing uses write() and writelines(). The with statement is the preferred way to work with files because it automatically closes the file, even if an exception occurs. File pointer movement is important when reading sequentially. Some files can be navigated using seek() and tell(). w mode truncates the file if it already exists. a mode appends to the end of the file. r mode requires the file to exist. MCQ traps: forgetting to close files can leak resources; w can erase content; and binary mode is needed for non-text data like images or PDFs.

sections.title: Error Handling & Exceptions
sections.content: Python handles errors using try, except, else, and finally. try contains code that may fail. except handles exceptions. else runs only if no exception occurs. finally runs whether an exception occurs or not, and is usually used for cleanup. raise is used to manually trigger an exception. Multiple except blocks can handle different exception types. A single except can catch a tuple of exceptions. Built-in exception examples include ValueError, TypeError, IndexError, KeyError, ZeroDivisionError, FileNotFoundError, and AttributeError. The exception hierarchy starts with BaseException. Most application exceptions inherit from Exception. SystemExit, KeyboardInterrupt, and GeneratorExit are special cases under BaseException. Custom exceptions can be created by subclassing Exception or one of its subclasses. Assertions using assert are often used for debugging and sanity checks; they can raise AssertionError when a condition is false. MCQ traps: finally generally executes even if there is a return statement; except should be ordered from specific to general; and not all errors are exceptions, because syntax errors happen before execution.
